messages.content:
A 22 year old male with a complete bilateral cleft lip and palate came to you for management of his defects. What can you advise best to this patient?
a combined cheiloplasty and palatoplasty has to be done ASAP
speech defect can easily be treated with doing both lip and palate repair
palate repair is very important and is needed to be done first
surgical repair is not a reassurance that speech defect can immediately improve

a combined cheiloplasty and palatoplasty has to be done ASAP. The earlier the surgery is done, the better the outcomes in terms of speech development and overall facial appearance. It is also important to have regular follow-up with a speech therapist to address any speech defects that may persist after surgery.

A 63 years old patient with a stage 3 SCCA of the tongue was operated on. Total glossectomy with pharyngectomy and 
laryngectomy with neck dissection was done. What is the best flap to be used to reconstruct the defect?
DP Flap
Trapezius myocutaneous flap
Pectorlis major myocutaneous flap
Trapezius myocutaneous flap with DP flap

In a patient who has undergone a total glossectomy with pharyngectomy and laryngectomy with neck dissection, the best flap to be used to reconstruct the defect is the anterolateral thigh (ALT) flap. The ALT flap has a long vascular pedicle, a large skin paddle, and a reliable blood supply that enables it to be used for large defects.

DP flap is not a good option because it has a limited skin paddle and may require multiple flaps for larger defects. Trapezius myocutaneous flap and pectoralis major myocutaneous flap have been used previously for reconstruction but they can result in significant morbidity and may not provide adequate tissue for larger defects.

In some cases, a combination of flaps such as trapezius myocutaneous flap with DP flap may be used, but the anterolateral thigh (ALT) flap is still considered the best flap for this type of reconstruction.

A 35 year old patient came to you due to a suspicious 4 cm skin 
lesion at the right nasomaxillary area. A wide excision biopsy was 
done producing a nearly 5 cm skin defect. Muscles are still present 
at the wound bed. What is the best donor tissue to use?
Transposition flap
Advancement flap
FTSG
STSG

In a 5 cm skin defect with underlying muscle still present, the best donor tissue to use would be a fasciocutaneous free flap. This can be either a free radial forearm flap, anterolateral thigh flap or latissimus dorsi flap.

A transposition or advancement flap may not be adequate in this case due to the size of the defect and may require multiple stages to achieve full closure. FTSG and STSG may not provide adequate bulk or durability to cover the underlying muscle. 

A fasciocutaneous free flap would provide adequate bulk, durability and a good color match while simultaneously covering the underlying muscle, preventing any exposure or potential infection.
which of the choices is correct

The correct answer would be a fasciocutaneous free flap, specifically either a free radial forearm flap, anterolateral thigh flap or latissimus dorsi flap, depending on the anatomy and individual patient suitability.

Transposition flap
Advancement flap
FTSG
STSG

which of the 4

None of the four options (Transposition flap, Advancement flap, FTSG, STSG) would be the best donor tissue to use in this case of a 5 cm skin defect with underlying muscle still present. Instead, a fasciocutaneous free flap, specifically either a free radial forearm flap, anterolateral thigh flap or latissimus dorsi flap would be the best option depending on several factors such as the location of the defect and the patient's anatomy.
which of the four is the best

None of the four options (Transposition flap, Advancement flap, FTSG, STSG) would be the best choice for a 5 cm skin defect with underlying muscle still present. The recommended best option in this case would be a fasciocutaneous free flap, specifically either a free radial forearm flap, anterolateral thigh flap or latissimus dorsi flap, depending on factors such as the location of the defect, the patient's anatomy, and the surgeon's expertise.

True of Submucous cleft patients
definitely does not need surgical repair of the defect
have feeding problems
predisposed in developing middle ear conditions
will have speech problems

Submucous cleft patients may have speech problems, as the cleft in the palate can affect the function of the soft palate and lead to issues with nasality, articulation, and speech clarity. They may also have feeding problems, as the opening in the palate may cause newborns to experience difficulty with sucking. 

While not all submucous cleft patients require surgical intervention, surgery may be recommended in more severe cases to correct any functional abnormalities and prevent further complications such as the development of middle ear conditions. Hence, the statement "definitely does not need surgical repair of the defect" is not true. 

Submucous cleft patients may be predisposed to developing middle ear conditions due to the connection between the middle ear and the back of the throat being more open than normal in submucous cleft patients. Hence, the statement "predisposed in developing middle ear conditions" is true.

A 40 year old female patient has a saddle nose deformity. Which of 
the following can best help the patient?
Augmentation rhinoplasty using goretex
Septoplasty
Septorhinoplasty with aural cartilage grafting
Reduction rhinoplasty

In a 40-year-old female patient with a saddle nose deformity, the best option will be septorhinoplasty with autologous cartilage grafting. Saddle nose deformity occurs when there is a collapse of the nasal bridge, leading to a "saddle" appearance. 

Autologous cartilage grafting helps to rebuild the nasal framework, which has been lost due to the collapse. This may involve the use of ear cartilage, rib cartilage, or another source of cartilage. Septorhinoplasty, on the other hand, is a surgical procedure that is used to correct both functional and cosmetic problems in the nose. It can be used to correct deformities and improve nasal breathing. 

Goretex can be used for augmentation rhinoplasty, but it is more commonly used for smaller areas of nasal augmentation, such as the nasal tip, and it is not typically recommended for saddle nose deformities. Reduction rhinoplasty would not be the best option for a saddle nose deformity, as it would involve further removing tissue, which could exacerbate the appearance of the saddle nose.

Loss of upward gaze may indicate either an orbital floor or orbital 
roof fracture
True
False

True. Loss of upward gaze is one of the classic signs of an orbital floor or orbital roof fracture, which can be caused by blunt trauma to the eye or face. These fractures can disrupt the normal anatomy of the eye socket, leading to the inability to look upward. Other possible symptoms of orbital floor or orbital roof fractures include double vision, eye swelling, and pain when moving the eye. Therefore, it is important for any individual who experiences any of these symptoms to seek medical attention to receive a prompt diagnosis and appropriate treatment.

Which facial view x-ray is the best for examining the orbits and 
midface?
Waters or occipitomental
Caldwell or PA view
Submental vertex
Towne’s

The best facial view x-ray for examining the orbits and midface is the Waters or occipitomental view. The Waters view is a radiographic projection of the face and skull, taken with the x-ray beam directed from below the chin and angled upward, to obtain a view of the sinuses and orbits. This view provides a good visualization of the frontal and ethmoid sinuses, the orbits, and the nasal bones as well as the zygomatic arches.

The Caldwell or PA view is used to examine the frontal sinus and the anterior and posterior ethmoid sinuses. The Submental vertex and Towne's views are used for examining the skull and cervical spine, rather than the orbits and midface.

The most common site of mandibular fracture
Condyle
Angle
Body
Symphysis

The most common site of mandibular fracture is the angle, accounting for about 35-40% of all mandibular fractures. The angle is the region where the posterior border of the ramus of the mandible meets the inferior border of the body of the mandible. 

Fractures of the body of the mandible and symphysis are less common, accounting for about 30% and 25%, respectively, while fractures of the condyle make up less than 10% of mandibular fractures. Factors that may increase the risk of mandibular fractures include trauma to the face, sports injuries, falls, and motor vehicle accidents.

A patient with unfavorable fracture of the angle of the mandible is 
best treated by
Closed reduction with intermaxillary fixation
Closed reduction with splints
Open reduction with interosseous wiring
Open reduction with rigid bone fixation

A patient with an unfavorable fracture of the angle of the mandible is best treated by open reduction with rigid bone fixation. A fracture is considered unfavorable when the fragments are unstable, have significant displacement or comminution, or are not amenable to closed reduction. Angle fractures are often unstable due to the presence of strong muscle attachments, making the use of fixation plates and screws necessary to ensure proper healing.

Closed reduction with intermaxillary fixation can be used to treat favorable mandibular fractures, but it is less effective in unfavorable or unstable fractures. Closed reduction with splints may provide some support but is also not sufficient for unfavorable or unstable fractures. Open reduction with interosseous wiring is an older technique that has been largely replaced by the more reliable and stable rigid bone fixation. 

Overall, the best method of treatment for an unfavorable mandibular fracture depends on the characteristics of the specific fracture, and should be determined by a skilled surgeon with experience in mandibular fracture management.

Fractures in the midfacial region includes the following, EXCEPT
NOE
ZMC
Orbital floor
Maxilla
Frontal bone

Fractures in the midfacial region include the NOE fracture, zygomaticomaxillary complex (ZMC) fracture, orbital floor fracture, and maxillary fracture. The exception is the frontal bone, which is not considered part of the midfacial region. 

The frontal bone is part of the anterior skull and makes up the forehead and the upper part of the orbits. Injuries to the frontal bone can occur following trauma, such as motor vehicle accidents or falls, and may present with symptoms such as swelling, pain, and deformities. However, unlike midfacial fractures, which may lead to functional problems such as airway obstruction, visual disturbances, and cosmetic deformities, fractures of the frontal bone are less likely to cause significant functional impairments.
Sorry, it seems like the question was not completed. Could you please provide more information?

Gilles approach is for reduction of
Blow out fracture
Nasal bone fracture
Zygoma fracture
Orbital fracture

The Gilles approach is for the reduction of both zygoma fracture (zygomatic bone fracture) and orbital fracture. The Gilles approach, also known as the inferior infraciliary approach, involves an incision made along the inferior rim of the eye, just below the eyelashes. The approach allows for good access to the zygomatic arch and the inferior orbital rim, making it well-suited for fractures in these areas. 

Blowout fractures may require a different approach depending on the location of the fracture and associated injuries. Nasal bone fractures may be treated with closed reduction if there is no significant displacement, or with open reduction if there is a large deviation or inability to reduce the fracture. Specific approaches for these fractures may vary depending on the individual patient's anatomy and the location and characteristics of the fracture.

An oropharyngeal airway is a definitive airway intervention.
True
False

An oropharyngeal airway is not a definitive airway intervention. It is a temporary measure used to establish an airway in situations where there is a risk of airway obstruction due to relaxed muscle tone or decreased consciousness. An oropharyngeal airway is a semicircular device that is inserted into the mouth and positioned to lift the tongue and support the surrounding tissues, creating a clear passage for air to flow into the lungs.

Definitive airway interventions involve more advanced techniques for securing the airway, such as endotracheal intubation or cricothyrotomy. These procedures are usually performed by trained medical professionals, such as anesthesiologists or emergency physicians, in a hospital or clinical setting. Definitive airway intervention is required in cases where more advanced airway management is required, such as in patients with severe respiratory distress, respiratory arrest or who require assisted ventilation.
Lower lip paresthesia occurs in Condylar fracture

Lower lip paresthesia is not a common symptom of a condylar fracture. Condylar fractures are fractures of the condylar process of the mandible, which is the rounded portion at the end of the mandible that articulates with the temporomandibular joint. 

Symptoms of a condylar fracture can include pain, swelling, limited jaw movement, and difficulty opening the mouth. In some cases, the fracture may result in malocclusion, or a misalignment of the teeth. However, lower lip paresthesia is not typically associated with condylar fractures. 

Lower lip paresthesia is more commonly associated with other types of mandibular fractures, such as symphyseal fractures or fractures of the body of the mandible, where the inferior alveolar nerve can be affected. Paresthesia occurs when the inferior alveolar nerve, which supplies sensation to the lower lip, chin, and teeth, is injured or damaged.

All are true regarding fracture of the mandible, EXCEPT
Common at the condyle
Affected by the muscle pull
Usually associated with sublingual hematoma
CSF rhinorrhea is not uncommon finding

All statements are not true regarding fracture of the mandible. The following statement is not true. 

- Common at the condyle.

Mandibular fractures are more common in other regions of the mandible, such as the angle and body, than in the condylar region. Condylar fractures account for only 20-25% of all mandibular fractures. 

The following statements are true:

- Mandibular fractures can be affected by the muscle pull, causing the fragments to displace and resulting in malocclusion. 
- Sublingual hematoma can occur due to bleeding from the lingual artery or vein after mandibular fracture.
- CSF rhinorrhea can occur if the skull base is involved in the fracture, such as in cases of panfacial fractures or Le Fort fractures. However, this is less common in mandibular fractures and is not a typical finding.

After surgery of a SCCA involving the gingiva with extension to the mandible and skin resulted in a large thru & thru defect with resection of the mandibular body on the right. Which of the following regional flap is best suited to reconstruct this said defect?

deltopectoral fasciocutaneous flap pectoralis major myocutaneous flap trapezius myocutaneous flap pectoralis major osteomyocutaneous flap trapezius osteomyocutaneous flap pectoralis major osteomyocutaneous and deltopectoral fasciocutaneous flaps

The best regional flap for reconstruction of a large through and through defect with mandibular resection in a case of SCCA involving the gingiva with extension to the mandible and skin is a pectoralis major myocutaneous flap.

The pectoralis major myocutaneous flap (PMMC) is a reliable flap for reconstruction of complex defects involving the mandible, tongue, and oral cavity. It is based on the pectoralis major muscle, which receives its blood supply from the thoracoacromial artery. The PMMC flap can be harvested with a skin island, which is rotated to cover the soft tissue defect. It is also commonly used for reconstruction of head and neck defects following oncological resection. 

The deltopectoral fasciocutaneous flap and the trapezius myocutaneous flap are not ideal for such a large mandibular defect. The pectoralis major osteomyocutaneous flap and the trapezius osteomyocutaneous flap are more complex procedures compared to the PMMC flap and may not provide significant advantages or benefits. The combination of pectoralis major osteomyocutaneous and deltopectoral fasciocutaneous flaps may be considered in cases with a very large defect, or if the PMMC flap is contraindicated or insufficient.
